summary: Martin Fowler documents his experiments using ChatGPT as a technical writing assistant for Thoughtworks' Technology Radar blips and macro trends articles. He finds the AI useful for creating first drafts and suggesting additional content, but notes significant weaknesses including overly optimistic tone, accuracy issues, and context limitations. The output requires substantial editing by skilled experts, and the time savings may be less than expected. He concludes that AI tools help humans produce higher quality results when used as collaborative starting points rather than replacements for human expertise.
key_points: ChatGPT produces content that is consistently too positive and optimistic, requiring editorial correction to achieve appropriate critical tone
AI-generated content needs review by skilled domain experts who can identify subtle errors and missing nuances
Context limitations and 'anchoring' behavior can cause ChatGPT to get stuck or forget earlier conversation details
Using context-setting prompts and treating the AI as a collaborative peer improves output quality
The productivity boost from AI may be smaller than expected since critical editing still takes significant time
money_quotes: ChatGPT output generally requires quite skilled experts to review and edit.
I don't think I saved time, overall, more that I was able to make steady progress by using the AI's output as a starting point.
I don't think the AIs are here to take our jobs—you still need a human expert working with the output—but I do expect these kinds of tools will help humans produce higher quality results.
It's surprisingly easy to become dependent on these AI tools.
Treat ChatGPT as a valuable peer rather than a subordinate.
The 'AI productivity boost' might not be as substantial as one might initially think.
Remember that, ultimately, it's your content and your name on the article.
ChatGPT is just way too optimistic and happy sounding, and almost ends up a little bit 'bland' because of this.
key_insight: AI writing assistants are most valuable as collaborative tools for creating first drafts and prompting additional ideas, but they require skilled human oversight to ensure accuracy and appropriate tone.
